hook: You already know how to multiply a matrix by a vector — but have you ever noticed that for certain special vectors, the matrix just stretches or flips them without changing their direction? Most vectors get knocked off course, yet these privileged ones stay on the same line through the origin, scaled by a single number. What makes these vectors so special, and why do they unlock some of the deepest secrets of a matrix?
concept: Every square matrix has a hidden skeleton — a set of preferred directions and scaling factors that reveal its long-term behavior, its geometry, and its structure. These are the eigenvalues and eigenvectors, unified formally by the equation $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda\mathbf{v}$.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to identify eigenvectors as the special nonzero vectors a matrix merely scales, and eigenvalues as the corresponding scaling factors satisfying $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda\mathbf{v}$.
Master the technique of computing eigenvalues by finding the roots of the characteristic polynomial $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$.
You will learn to factor a matrix as $A = PDP^{-1}$ when a full basis of eigenvectors exists, making powers and long-run behavior of the matrix dramatically easier to compute.
Understand why a repeated eigenvalue does not always yield enough eigenvectors, and use the relationship between geometric and algebraic multiplicity to determine when diagonalization fails.
Apply eigenvalue techniques to matrices with complex eigenvalues, interpreting conjugate pairs as encoding rotation-and-scaling behavior in the plane.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Applications: Discrete Dynamical Systems and Markov Chains
topic_roadmap.description: Apply diagonalization to model real-world systems that evolve in steps, predicting long-term steady states of population models and probability chains using dominant eigenvalues.

You will learn to decompose any matrix — even non-diagonalizable ones — into the nearly-diagonal Jordan canonical form $A = PJP^{-1}$, completing the full structural picture of a linear transformation.
